significant_corrections: John Stoney b. 1779 (was 1737); Elizabeth Gaillard (was Guilland); marriage at 'Rocks' plantation Dec 1805 (not Mont Repose 1766); 'Senate / Calhoun' career hallucinated — actually a 1838 death notice + a transcribed 1821 City Gazette obituary for John Stoney Sr. (d. age 73 at Head Island); right column is a Gaillard descent chart from immigrant Joachim Gaillard (b. 1625 Montpellier) down to the compiler Amy Perry FitzSimons; 'Mont Repose' was 'Montpellier'; 'Mary Mary Mary' garble was the chart's terminal generations resolving on the compiler herself

Portrait reidentified — was 'Bishop Gadsden', actually Rev. Nathaniel Bowen (printed caption); paperclipped typescript completely re-read as Porcher-Cordes-Gendron Huguenot ancestry (Capt. Peter Gaillard's wife Elizabeth Porcher 1760–1804, immigration from St. Severe/Bazamet c.1686); 'Pelis Guilland' = Peter Gaillard (Revolutionary War biography; educated in England, served Marion); Winyah spelling corrected from 'Wineyaw'; numerous Sinkler/Pinckney/Bryan ghost-names removed

Photograph identified as Nathalie Ferguson 1891 (annotation written sideways, recovered by rotation); Joseph Sanford Barlow died Nov. 6, 1844 (was 1849) in St. John's Berkeley (not Goose Creek); Henrietta Catherine Gaillard b. 1774 d. 1858; inscribed slip authorship corrected: granddaughter Ellen Milliken Barker who married Thomas Porcher (was 'Ellen Lillian Barlow ... Sherman Pinckney'); refugee envelope routes to Walhalla SC (not Wadesboro NC); full Barlow children list recovered

Letter date Sep 15th (was Sep 10th); 'salutation' was an envelope-stationery side-note, letter actually opens 'We had a Telegram...'; multiple name rescues — Judge Stoney→Judge Glover, Mrs Theodore Jones→Mrs Theodore Jervey, Eadis ville→Cordesville, Frank Cottenfields→Frank Campbell, 'on board Marshal Wing'→'on parole — Mitchel King told me'; date_range narrowed to early 1860s (Civil War context: Rapidan telegraph, parole, Mitchell King)

Multiple corrections — Judge Stoney→Judge Glover; Mrs Theodore Jones→Mrs Theodore Jervey; Edisto→Cordesville; Frank Cottenfields/Marshal Wing→Frank Campbell on parole; Eliza→Clara; teaching Nancy→teaching Grace; date_range narrowed to 1861–1865 (Civil War parole context with Mitchell King reference)

CASTLE HUGH TRACT→CASTLE RUIN TRACT; will date Feb 24 1882→1881; lower handwritten margin was hallucinated as a list of mortgages/banks (Theodore M. Barker as Trustee, W.M. Walker of Wilm. N.C., Sole Sales Bank, Henry Gourdin) — actually paragraph 8(d) describing 'Ellery' tract on Monck's Corner Road adjoining Mulberry Plantation, descended from Thomas Milliken; added C.W. Olsen, Thomas Milliken, Adam T. Milliken, James Tupper to people

Subject identified — Theodore Gaillard Barker (not Garland); father Samuel Gaillard Barker; mother Ellen Milliken (not Esther Pickens, who does not appear); wife Louisa Preston King daughter of Judge Mitchell King (not Susan Pickens Barker); childless couple; cited publication 'A Day on Cooper River' by John B. Irving M.D., enlarged by Louisa Cheves Stoney (was 'A Story on Coosaw River by Captain Henry')

Left leaf recovered in full — passage on subject's character rising 'superior to physical disadvantages'; previous reading 'His good [intent?] was an unsleeping desire' was a hallucination, actual text recovered as 'His genial Nature was a wonderful defence against morbid egotism'; right leaf re-read as passage on 'the ruined country' and subject's earlier happier days; only 1 illegibility marker remaining (was 45)

Left leaf, originally mass of [illegible] placeholders, resolved into continuous prose — panegyric on Dr. Christopher FitzSimons's moral character (earnest denunciation of wrong, indignation against falsehood/trickery); manner of death recovered: 'him crushed beneath the ruins of a falling house' — recasts document from generic obituary to eulogy following accidental building collapse; interlinear 'warm' between 'an' and 'advocate' detected at high resolution

Entire page is biographical account of Dr. Christopher FitzSimons (b. 1828, d. 1866 in tornado at Moss Grove plantation, St. John's-Berkeley) — previous transcription completely missed this and invented unrelated narrative about an 1825 Gadsden-officiated wedding, an 1899 Gulielmo Barker letter, and a 'Sycamore plantation' (none on page); opening reference is to Rhett capturing Stede Bonnet (1718 Charleston pirate trial), not 'O.M. Brouett'; right column is verbatim Charleston Courier 'Fatal Tornado' obituary of May 19, 1866; majority of previous people-list entries (James Harold, John S. Tarry, Cashel/Paul FitzSimons, Andrew Pearcy, Capt. Andrew Barker, Gulielmo Barker, Theodore G. Barker) do not appear on the page

Letter dated Hendersonville NC Aug 8 1944 (was 1899); written by Gaillard Stoney FitzSimons to Amy FitzSimons Walker; full migration recovered: Charleston→Flat Rock (fled yellow fever 1867/1870)→Spartanburg→Charlotte NC (1875)→Mills River (bought farm 1876); 'Elliott House' was the rented house in Flat Rock (not a person); Carolina Military Institute (C.M.I.) where Uncle Kit and the writer's father Seaman were boarding cadets; parenthetical confirms Amy FitzSimons Walker born Feb 4 1888 in the Green Hill St. Charlotte house

Grantor of underlying 1869 deed is Thomas Morris (not James Morris), recorded Book 6 Page 552 dated 22 Oct 1869; pencil annotations name James C. Evans and S. K. King dated 31 March 1866 (not 'James C. Morris' and 'Thomas L. King'); lower-left note is a clerk's standard interlineation/erasure certificate, not a separate transaction; date_range extended to 1866–1876

Confirmed NOT cross-written (perpendicular text is upper-margin overflow, not a full second layer); letter from Walhalla, June 1st; Brother Frances→Brother Theodore (Stoney); diggings→druggist's; Cuba→Columbia; Friday→Tuesday; Mrs. Davis→Mr. Dewees (with daughter Addy Dewees); Mrs. Sandy King→Mrs. Henry King; 'Capt. [illegible]'→Capt. Cam Evans; Dr. Miles recovered; removed Frank Campbell/Pomerie/Lieut. Coleman hallucinations

Garland→Gaillard (Theodore Gaillard Barker, Samuel Gaillard Barker — propagating the 240.md correction); Esther Pickens Barker misreading → Susan Milliken Barker; wife Louisa Preston King daughter of Judge Mitchell King; citation 'A Story on Coosaw River by Captain Henry'→'A Day on Cooper River' by John B. Irving M.D. enlarged by Louisa Cheves Stoney; right column reframed as separate anecdote — conversation between Aunt Louisa and an old woman comparing Uncle Theodore to Cardinal Richelieu / Napoleon Bonaparte (not biography)

Letter narrates the death of Mrs. Milliken (named on p. 263), addressed to her daughter Mollie; Phase-1's 'heavily cross-hatched, three orientations, largely illegible' wrong — upper 3/4 is standard single-orientation cursive, only bottom quarter is a 90°-rotated supplement in the same hand; recovered names: Dr. Moultrie, Dr. Bellinger, Mrs. Northrop, Uncle Eddie; clinical detail 'inflammation of the Kidneys', death 'at 8 o'clock the morning after', Dr. Bellinger 'it was out of human skill'

Mounted slip is 'message from Gen'l. Wade Hampton' (not 'passage from grand uncle Hampton' — 'Gen'l. Wade' misread as 'grand uncle'); pencil text is a comic valentine verse, not a reminiscence — closing line '(A Valentine sent to Grandfather and dictated to me by Allie - A.A.W.)' confirms compiler dictated by 'Allie' to Dr. Christopher FitzSimons (3rd); lower photo has TWO captions (above + below) with initials G.S.F.S./C.F.S./S.G.F./S.S.F.S.; dropped spurious Amy A.S./A. Fuller/A.A.W./Mrs. Seaman FitzSimons entries

Page is printed newsprint with illustration, not cursive — 75+ original [illegible] markers were avoidable; phantom 'Reverdy' family doesn't exist on page; wedding takes place at home of Rawlins Lowndes; bridegroom is Francis Simmons; illustration caption is 'Burying her face in the pillows, little Ruth wept her heart out...' (was 'Resting her face on the pillow'); marital home address 131 Tradd Street, Charleston, recovered

Confirmed NOT cross-written — heavily stained but single-orientation; continues page 093's Walhalla letter; 'Brother Theodore' confirmed; Joe Huger named as 'prisoner in the Bermuda' (likely Bermuda Hundred campaign May/June 1864), exchanged, passed through Greenville; 'I am so glad she has heard at last' (was hallucinated 'Hunter is glad'); 'Fannie [Wigfall/Trapier?] is a great deal better — has lost her fever today' (was spurious 'two fits today'); 'lots of spoilt children' confirmed

Major place correction — 'Naranchee Valley'→Nacoochee Valley (White County, Georgia, real mid-19th-c. mountain resort); 'My appetite is nearly well of the scarlet fever' was nonsense → 'Miss Wigfall is nearly well of the Scarlet fever'; 'Nelle weighs a dozen pounds one week' fabrication → 'Nellie is well & except a sore Mouth one Week'; 'I make Cous a hot toddy mug'→'I make Grace boil Soft rice for his breakfast' (Grace is the cook); 'little pigs & gulls running in this house'→'little boys & girls living in this house' (hotel context); 'salts'→Kate's

Civil War letter c. 1861–62 (Seward reference dates it — 'Europe will frighten Seward & co into Sanity', referring to U.S. Secretary of State William H. Seward 1861–69); many object-misread-as-person corrections — 'Mrs [Lemke?]'→'his trunk'; '[Pickle?] & Mr [Mosquitoes?]'→'cool nights & no Mosquitoes' (weather, not people); '[Pat?] & [grandfather?] & [uncertain] returns of the day'→'Kate & yourself & happy returns of the day'; '[Martha?] [uncertain] [Theodore?] will [seem?]'→'Most for those little ones'; '[Kemp?] on Stationary'→'stamps or Stationary'; Mr Brownfield (was Mr Broomfield)

Letter from Walhalla SC, '12 June', addressed 'Dear William'; '[free?] from yourself'→'kind'; '[could?] I [would?] order to-day'→'Father said I must write to-day'; 'Blacksville'→Clarksville (Charles travels there with Father); 'He hoped he has [sought?] [unc]o [Briggs?] & [Barry?] our landlord'→'The horse he has bought, the rest [Buggy & harness, need from] our Landlord'; 'a [Barr?] of [Mules?]'→'a pair of Mules'; Mr Ed Perronneau confirmed; 'Sily makes the girls wait'→'Solly washes & the girls wait'; 'to held him & Wm to Wm the Bellows'→'to weld iron & Wm to blow the Bellows'

date_range corrected from 1853 → 1862–1864 (Confederate Charleston/Greenville indicators: 'Refugees come daily', 'Ladies shoes are now $40 & $45 & May rise from that', hotel closures); 'Laurie & horses are now $40 & $45'→'Ladies shoes are now $40 & $45' (key wartime detail misread; Laurie removed from people); 'Sho & Wm'→'Theo & Wm' (Theodore and William); 'Mrs Bingle Clarke'→'Mr Pringle Clarke' (Charleston family); 'Marion & Minnie'→'Mart. & Henne' (different abbreviated forms); 'the depot girl Nell'→'The school girls'; 'If we could or she name of got worse'→'If we [should be?] snow-bound this winter!'
